figures.caption: Fig. 3. Interval-based surface separation. Surfels are sorted by their interval start 𝑧start. They are merged into surface groups when their depth intervals overlap. A gap between the running group maximum and the next surfel’s interval start finalizes the current layer and begins a new one.
Table 1. Inverse rendering comparison on Stanford-ORB. †denotes models trained with point-sampled objects from the ground-truth 3D scans. Depth SI-MSE ×10−3. Shape Chamfer distance 2 × 10−3.

Fig. 5. Qualitative inverse rendering comparison on Stanford-ORB.We compare novel view synthesis, surface normal estimation, and novel scene relighting against NVDiffRec [Munkberg et al. 2022] and ground truth. 3DSS recovers smoother normals and more faithful specular highlights under novel illumination.

Fig. 10. A point-sampled checkerboard plane (4M surfels) rendered at 800×600. (c) Without MIP filtering, the unfiltered reconstruction kernel cannot suppress high frequencies, producing Moiré artifacts that worsen toward the horizon. (a)Our center-precomputed MIP filter eliminates aliasing at a cost of only 8ms over the unfiltered baseline, producing results visually indistinguishable from (b) the per-pixel Jacobian evaluation of AA-2DGS. (d) 4× supersampling serves as a reference but doubles the rendering time.

Fig. 6. Mesh extraction on Stanford-ORB. Ground-truth laser scans (left) compared with NVDiffRec (DMTet extraction) and two pathways from our surfel representation: TSDF fusion of rendered depth maps, and SPSR applied directly to the oriented point cloud.

Fig. 2. Rendering pipeline of 3D Surface Splatting (3DSS): custom operations in red, intermediate buffers in green, and learnable or discrete inputs on the left. (1) Preprocessing. Each surfel is transformed to view space and its T-matrix is constructed for analytic ray–surfel intersection and clip-space bounding.(2) Forward shading. Every surfel is shaded before rasterization via split-sum IBL against the co-optimized HDR environment, yielding a pre-shaded linear radiance c𝑘 . (3) Tiling & sorting. Surfels are binned into screen-space tiles and radix-sorted by the depth-interval start 𝑧start. (4) Per-pixel rasterization. For each pixel, viewing rays are intersected with each surfel, a MIP-filtered EWA kernel weight 𝑤 is evaluated at the hit, overlapping depth intervals are merged into surface layers, weighted contributions are normalized, and a smooth coverage drives front-to-back compositing across all layers. (5) Output. The composited HDR image is blended with the background then tone-mapped and gamma-corrected. Gradients flow back through all operations to update the surfel parameters and the environment map.
Table 2. Ablation study on a representative subset of 7 object–scene pairs. Converged models are calibrated to ∼100K surfels. Albedo PSNR-L is measured against pseudo ground-truth albedo.
